slides.title: Find Patterns in Any Text
slides.body: A **regular expression** (regex) is a tiny pattern language that describes what text to look for. Instead of dozens of `if`-statements, one pattern can find emails, phone numbers, or prices in a single line.
slides.tip: Python's built-in `re` module is all you need â€” no install required.

slides.title: Import re and Start Searching
slides.body: Use `re.search(pattern, text)` to find the first match anywhere in a string. It returns a **Match object** if found, or `None` if not.
slides.code:
import re

text = "My email is hello@example.com"
result = re.search(r"hello", text)
print(result.group())
slides.tip: Always write patterns as raw strings `r"..."` so backslashes pass through to regex unchanged.

slides.title: Metacharacters = Pattern Power
slides.body: Special symbols let you describe **types** of characters, not just specific ones. `\d` matches any digit, `\w` matches any word character, and `+` means "one or more".
slides.code:
import re

text = "I have 3 cats and 12 dogs"
print(re.findall(r"\d+", text))
slides.tip: `findall` returns a list of **all** matches â€” perfect for grabbing every number, word, or email at once.

slides.title: Four Functions Cover Everything
slides.body: `re.search` finds the first match, `re.findall` collects all matches, `re.sub` replaces matches, and `re.match` checks if the string **starts** with the pattern.
slides.code:
import re

text = "Prices: $10, $250, $3"
censored = re.sub(r"\$\d+", "???", text)
print(censored)
slides.tip: Use `re.search` (not `re.match`) when the pattern could appear anywhere in the text.

slides.title: Groups Let You Extract Pieces
slides.body: Wrap part of a pattern in `()` to **capture** that piece separately. Call `.group(1)`, `.group(2)`, etc. to pull out each captured chunk.
slides.code:
import re

match = re.search(r"(\w+)@([\w.]+)", "hi billing@company.org")
print(match.group(1))
print(match.group(2))
slides.tip: Always guard with `if match:` before calling `.group()` â€” `re.search` returns `None` when nothing is found.

slides.title: You Know Regex Now!
slides.body: You can import `re`, write raw-string patterns using metacharacters like `\d` and `\w`, and use `search`, `findall`, `sub`, and groups to slice and dice any text. For simple lookups, plain `str` methods are still cleaner â€” save regex for genuinely complex patterns.
slides.tip: Regex can't parse HTML or JSON â€” use `BeautifulSoup` or `json.loads()` for those.
